Space Unit · Explanation Writing

Which Planet Could Humans Live On?

A journey through the most promising worlds in the known universe — and the surprising places where life might be possible.

TRAPPIST-1 e Mars LHS 1140 b Proxima b

Have you ever looked up at the night sky and thought about which planet humans could live on? While most planets in the universe are gas giants or frozen rocks, astronomers have discovered a few special worlds that might be perfect for us.

01 — Our Red Next-Door Neighbor

Mars

Mars is the most likely planet for humans to live on next. We can travel there in aboout 7 months using current rocket technology.

Mars has a day length of 24 hours and 37 minutes, which is very similar to Earth. This means humans and plants can easily adjust there sleep cycles.

Mars at a Glance
Day Length
24h 37m
Surface Temp
−63 °C
Atmosphere
95% CO₂

Mars is freezing and its atmosphere is too thin to hold heat or liquid water — but scientists believe we could fix this through terraforming.

However, Mars is freezing cold (−63 °C) and the atmosphere is very thin (less than 1% of Earth's). It is also 95% CO₂, so we cannot breathe the air.

To fix this, scientists like Elon Musk believe we can use terraforming (warming the planet and making the atmosphere thicker) until humans can live there.

02 — 🌌 Beyond Our Solar System

The Exoplanets

If we look outside our solar system, we find exoplanets. Scientists look for planets in the Habitable Zone (or "Goldilocks Zone"), where temperatures are just right so water can stay liquid.

The Habitable Zone

The "Goldilocks zone" — the narrow band around a star where conditions allow liquid water to exist on a planet's surface.

Proxima Centauri b: This is the closest exoplanet to us. However, it's star shoots out violent flares. The dangerous radiation has likely stripped away its atmosphere, making it a hard place for life to survive.

TRAPPIST-1 e: This is an Earth-sized, rocky planet. However, it is tidaly locked, meaning one side always faces its sun (always day) and the other faces away (always night). Humans would have to live in the narrow "twilight strip" between the extreme temperature differences.

03 — 👁️ The Eyeball Planet

LHS 1140 b

LHS 1140 b is a Super-Earth with a very unique nickname: the "Eyeball Planet."

The Eyeball Planet

A frozen world with a giant melted ocean facing its star like a pupil — recent James Webb data hints at a nitrogen atmosphere similar to Earth's.

The Eyeball Shape: It is also tidally locked. Because it is covered in ice, the side facing the star melts into a permanent ocean, while the rest stays frozen. From space, it looks like an eye.

New Discovery: Recent data from the James Webb Space Telescope suggests it might have a thick nitrogen atmosphere, similar Earth's. The liquid water on the day side could be around 20 °C, making it one of the best contenders for alien life.

Key Vocabulary

Words To Know

Atmosphere
The layer of gases surrounding a planet. On Earth, it allows us to breathe and protects us from the sun.
Habitable Zone
The "Goldilocks" area around a star where it is not too hot and not too cold, allowing liquid water to exist.
Tidally Locked
When a planet takes the same amount of time to spin as it does to orbit, causing one side to always face the star.
Super-Earth
An exoplanet that has a mass higher than Earth's, but smaller than giants like Neptune.
Terraforming
The process of deliberately changing a planet's temperature and atmosphere to make it more like Earth — so humans can live there.
